markdown.markdown:
**Find the right statistical method for your analytical question.**

This page provides the fastest route to the right method for experienced analysts. If you're new to statistical analysis, start with [@sec-getting-started].

::: {.callout-tip icon=false}

Not sure which method applies, or hitting an edge case? The **Quantitative Analytics and Statistics Guild** is your first port of call — Teams channels, monthly meetings, and Quant Clinic drop-in sessions. See [When to Seek Specialist Support](#when-to-seek-specialist-support) below, or go to @sec-getting-started for fuller guidance.
:::

## Method Selection Flowchart

Use this flowchart to find the right method for your question. Start at the top.

```{mermaid}
flowchart TD
    A([What is your analytical question?]) --> B{Comparing providers\nor groups?}
    A --> C{Monitoring over time?}
    A --> D{Exploring a relationship?}
    A --> E{Data quality or\nexploration?}

    B --> B1{How many providers?}
    B1 -->|Many vs national benchmark| B2[Z-scoring\n@sec-z-scoring]
    B1 -->|Exactly two groups| B3{Paired or independent?}
    B3 -->|Paired — same units\nbefore and after| B4[Paired t-test\n@sec-t-tests]
    B3 -->|Independent — different units| B5[Two-sample t-test\n@sec-t-tests]

    C --> C1{What type of data?}
    C1 -->|Continuous — one\nvalue per period| C2[I-chart SPC\n@sec-spc-basics]
    C1 -->|Proportion per period| C3[P-chart SPC\n@sec-spc-basics]
    C1 -->|Count with\nconstant denominator| C4[C-chart SPC\n@sec-spc-basics]
    C1 -->|Rate with\nvarying denominator| C5[U-chart SPC\n@sec-spc-basics]

    D --> D1[Correlation analysis\n@sec-correlation]

    E --> E2{What is the problem?}
    E2 -->|Extreme values to flag| E3[Outlier detection\n@sec-outlier-methods]
    E2 -->|Missing values| E4[Missing data methods\n@sec-missing-data-handling]
    E2 -->|Understand data structure| E5[Data types\n@sec-data-types]
    E2 -->|Describe distributions| E6[Describing data\n@sec-describing-data]

    style B2 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745
    style B4 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745
    style B5 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745
    style C2 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085
    style C3 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085
    style C4 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085
    style C5 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085
    style D1 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404
    style E3 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24
    style E4 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24
    style E5 fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#383d41
    style E6 fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#383d41
```

## Common Analyst Scenarios

**Jump directly to the section you need:**

| I need to... | Go to Section |
|--------------|---------------|
| Compare one provider to national average | Z-scoring [@sec-z-scoring] |
| Compare many providers to national average | Z-scoring [@sec-z-scoring] |
| Track performance over time | SPC Basics [@sec-spc-basics] |
| Detect when a change occurred | SPC Basics [@sec-spc-basics] |
| Compare two groups of providers | t-tests [@sec-t-tests] |
| Test before/after an intervention | t-tests [@sec-t-tests] |
| Understand relationship between two measures | Correlation [@sec-correlation] |
| Identify extreme outliers systematically | Outlier Methods [@sec-outlier-methods] |
| Investigate unusual values | Unusual Observations [@sec-unusual-observations] |
| Handle missing data | Missing Data Methods [@sec-missing-data-handling] |
| Understand why data is missing | Missing Data Foundations [@sec-missing-data-foundations] |
| Set up QA documentation | QA Principles [@sec-qa-principles] |
| Understand my data type | Data Types [@sec-data-types] |
| Calculate summary statistics | Describing Data [@sec-describing-data] |
| Understand confidence intervals | Statistical Inference [@sec-statistical-inference] |

## Method Selection

| Your Question | Method | When to Use | Chapter |
|---------------|--------|-------------|---------|
| Compare many providers to benchmark | **Z-scoring** | 10+ providers vs national average | 10 [@sec-z-scoring] |
| Monitor performance over time | **SPC charts** | Detect changes in time series | 11 [@sec-spc-basics] |
| Compare two groups | **t-tests** | Pilot vs control, before vs after | 12 [@sec-t-tests] |
| Explore relationships | **Correlation** | Association between variables | 13 [@sec-correlation] |
| Investigate unusual values | **Outlier methods** | Extreme observations requiring investigation | 14 [@sec-outlier-methods] |
| Handle missing data | **Missing data methods** | Substantial missingness | 15 [@sec-missing-data-handling] |

## By Data Type and Sample Size

**Choose your method based on what you're measuring and how much data you have:**

| Data Type | Sample Size | Comparison Type | Recommended Method | Chapter |
|-----------|-------------|-----------------|-------------------|---------|
| **Continuous** | Any | Single provider vs benchmark | Z-scoring | [@sec-z-scoring] |
| **Continuous** | Any | Many providers vs benchmark | Z-scoring | [@sec-z-scoring] |
| **Continuous** | ≥30 per group | Two groups | t-test (independent) | [@sec-t-tests] |
| **Continuous** | ≥30 pairs | Before/after (paired) | t-test (paired) | [@sec-t-tests] |
| **Continuous** | Time series | Trend detection | SPC (I-chart) | [@sec-spc-basics] |
| **Continuous** | ≥30 | Relationship between variables | Correlation | [@sec-correlation] |
| **Counts** | Time series | Trend detection | SPC (C-chart, U-chart) | [@sec-spc-basics] |
| **Counts** | ≥100 total | vs benchmark | Z-scoring (with continuity correction) | [@sec-z-scoring] |
| **Proportions** | ≥100 total | vs benchmark | Z-scoring | [@sec-z-scoring] |
| **Proportions** | Time series | Trend detection | SPC (P-chart) | [@sec-spc-basics] |
| **Binary** | ≥100 total | Two groups | t-test on proportions | [@sec-t-tests] |
| **Ordinal** | Any | Any | Raise with the Guild (specialised methods) | - |
| **Nominal** | Any | Group comparisons | Raise with the Guild (Chi-squared, etc.) | - |

**Notes: Sample sizes are guidelines, not rigid thresholds. See individual method chapters for detailed requirements.**

## Common Workflows

::: {.workflow-cards}
::: {.workflow-card .nav-card-examples}
**Profile Provider Performance**

1. Check data quality and data type ([@sec-data-types])
2. Explore distributions ([@sec-describing-data], [@sec-variation-uncertainty], [@sec-distribution-shape])
3. Apply z-scoring ([@sec-z-scoring])
4. Investigate unusual observations ([@sec-unusual-observations], [@sec-outlier-methods])
5. Document methods, assumptions, and limitations
:::

::: {.workflow-card .nav-card-foundations}
**Evaluate Intervention or Policy Change**

1. Check data quality, data type, and baseline comparability ([@sec-data-types])
2. Understand statistical inference principles ([@sec-statistical-inference])
3. Assess assumptions (normality, equal variance)
4. Apply paired or two-sample t-test ([@sec-t-tests])
5. Report effect size, confidence interval, and p-value
6. Interpret in context (practical vs statistical significance)
7. Document for QA review
:::

::: {.workflow-card .nav-card-methods}
**Monitor Trends and Detect Changes**

1. Check data quality, data type, and temporal completeness ([@sec-data-types])
2. Understand variation ([@sec-variation-uncertainty]) and common vs special cause
3. Select appropriate chart type based on data type ([@sec-spc-basics])
4. Create SPC chart with control limits
5. Identify special cause signals (rules-based or visual)
6. Investigate flagged periods (root cause, context)
7. Update baselines when sustained changes confirmed
:::
:::

## At a Glance: From Question to Analysis

The typical analytical workflow has three stages. You don't always need every step, but this is the general shape:

**1. Start with your question** — what are you trying to find out?

| I'm comparing... | Method |
|---|---|
| One or many providers vs benchmark | Z-scoring [@sec-z-scoring] |
| Performance over time | SPC [@sec-spc-basics] |
| Two groups | t-test [@sec-t-tests] |
| Two variables (relationship) | Correlation [@sec-correlation] |
| Investigating unusual values | Outlier Methods [@sec-outlier-methods] |

**2. Check your data** — before running any analysis, understand what you're working with.

Know your data type ([@sec-data-types]), check for quality issues ([@sec-qa-principles]), look at the distribution ([@sec-describing-data], [@sec-distribution-shape]), and assess whether you have enough data ([@sec-statistical-inference]). Check for unusual observations ([@sec-unusual-observations]) and assess missing data ([@sec-missing-data-foundations]).

**3. Run, interpret, document** — follow the method chapter step-by-step, check assumptions, interpret results in context, and record your methods, assumptions, limitations, and interpretation for QA ([@sec-qa-principles]).

## When to Seek Support from the Guild

Raise questions at the Quant Clinic or on the Guild Teams channel when:

- Designing new indicators or assessing fitness for purpose
- Complex missing data (>20% or MNAR suspected)
- Methodology challenged by providers or stakeholders
- Method unclear — the flowchart above doesn't give a clear answer
- Advanced methods needed: multilevel models, multiple imputation, causal inference, CUSUM/EWMA SPC

See [Getting Help: The Guild](getting-help.qmd) for Quant Clinic dates and contact details. For complex or high-stakes cases, escalate to the **[Practice Lead for Quantitative Analytics and Statistics](mailto:federico.andreis@cqc.org.uk)**.
